figures.caption: Figure 2: Experimental setting on nursery pig farm. (a-b) Schematic and real views of the camera-pen arrangement.

Figure 6: Pig detection examples by raw GroundingDINO under different conditions. In each panel, the left image shows the prediction result, and the right image shows the ground truth. Detection was generally more accurate in daytime color images than in nighttime grayscale images, and when pigs were more separated rather than stacked. (a) Daytime, active pigs. (b) Daytime, inactive pigs. (c) Nighttime, active pigs. (d) Nighttime, inactive pigs. (e) False positive: pig outside the pen. (f) False positive: partial pig detection.

Figure 1: (a) Conceptual comparison between conventional supervised learning and a foundation-model (FM)–based framework. (b) FM-based pipeline for pig video segmentation and tracking, including text-prompted detection, mask generation, and mask-based tracking across video frames.

Figure 5: Grounding-DINO performance for pig detection. (a) F1 score, recall, and precision across different Grounding DINO thresholds. (b) Hexbin histogram comparing recall and precision before post-evaluation. (c) Results after post-evaluation, TP, FP, and FN represent true positive, false positive, and false negative, respectively.

Figure 4: Long-term video segmentation workflow. The pipeline integrates six modules to extend short-term segmentation into long-term tracking across consecutive video clips: initializer (identity assignment from a clean reference frame), tracker (mask propagation within clips), matcher (cross-clip identity linking), mask refinement (removing duplicate or erroneous masks), re-identification (restoring missing IDs), and post-segmentation quality control (flagging unreliable results).

Figure 9: Quantitative results for long-term video segmentation. Modified foundation model performance using the J&F metric. (a) Mean J , F , and J&F scores over the time series. (b) J scores for each individual pig.

Figure 8: Short-term video segmentation results. (a) Number of stacked and unstacked pigs and the unstacked pig ratio across days and hours. and (b) Tracking errors within active pig tracks by raw Grounded-SMA2.

Figure 10: Qualitative results for long-term video segmentation. (a) The Initializer selects an anchor frame, the Tracker propagates masks forward and backward, and the Matcher ensures identity consistency across video clips. (b) More examples from other time points.

Figure 3: Bidirectional SAM2 propagation diagram. After identifying the first erroneous frame (red cross), GroundingDINO was used to find a valid anchor frame (green box), and SAM2 tracking was applied backward (purple arrow) and forward (blue arrow) to replace the erroneous segment with a corrected track.
Table 1: Tracking errors and video challenges during short-term video segmentation evaluation

Figure 11: Qualitative results for long-term video segmentation. The mask refinement module corrects errors by preventing masks from drifting outside the pen (pen boundary filter, shown in (a) and (b)) and from jumping or switching identities across frames (blob consistency filter, shown in (c) and (d)).

Figure 7: Short-term video segmentation examples by raw Grounded-SAM2. (a-b) Pigs outside of the pen. (c-d) Pigs piling up. (e-f) Incorrect masks. (g-h) Duplicated label for the same pig. (i-j) ID switch. (k-l) Lost track.
